flashcards.front: What are the three main types of computer networks classified by geographic scope?
flashcards.back: LAN (Local Area Network) covers a small area like a building or campus; MAN (Metropolitan Area Network) covers a city or large campus; WAN (Wide Area Network) spans countries or continents. LAN offers high speed and low latency; WAN provides broad geographic coverage but at lower speeds.

flashcards.front: Compare Bus, Star, Ring, and Mesh network topologies with respect to structure and data flow.
flashcards.back: Bus topology uses a single backbone cable with terminators at both ends; data travels in both directions. Star topology connects all nodes to a central hub/switch; data passes through the center. Ring topology connects nodes in a closed loop, typically using token passing. Mesh topology interconnects every node with multiple paths, offering high redundancy.

flashcards.front: List and explain the 7 layers of the OSI model in order.
flashcards.back: Physical (1) - transmits raw bits over media; Data Link (2) - provides node-to-node transfer with MAC addressing; Network (3) - handles routing and logical addressing (IP); Transport (4) - ensures reliable end-to-end delivery (TCP) or fast delivery (UDP); Session (5) - manages sessions between applications; Presentation (6) - handles data translation, encryption, compression; Application (7) - provides network services to user applications.

flashcards.front: What are the 4 layers of the TCP/IP model and how do they map to the OSI model?
flashcards.back: Application Layer (combines OSI 7,6,5) - interfaces with user applications; Transport Layer (OSI 4) - provides host-to-host communication; Internet Layer (OSI 3) - handles logical addressing and routing; Link Layer (OSI 2,1) - defines hardware addressing and transmission media. TCP/IP is the practical implementation that inspired the OSI theoretical framework.

flashcards.front: Explain the process of encapsulation and de-encapsulation in network communication.
flashcards.back: Encapsulation adds protocol headers at each OSI layer as data moves down: Application data → Segments (Transport) → Packets (Network) → Frames (Data Link) → Bits (Physical). De-encapsulation reverses this at the receiver: each layer removes its corresponding header, reads the payload, and passes data upward until the original application data is delivered.

flashcards.front: Compare connection-oriented (TCP) and connectionless (UDP) protocols in terms of reliability and overhead.
flashcards.back: TCP provides reliable, ordered delivery through handshaking (three-way handshake), flow control, and error correction, but incurs higher overhead and latency. UDP offers faster transmission with minimal overhead by sending packets without establishing a connection or guaranteeing delivery, suitable for real-time applications like video streaming and VoIP where speed is critical.

flashcards.front: What are the practical advantages and disadvantages of a Star topology?
flashcards.back: Advantages: easy to install and manage, simple troubleshooting (isolating node failures), scalable (adding/removing nodes without disrupting network). Disadvantages: dependent on the central hub/switch (if it fails, entire network goes down), requires more cabling than bus topology, and performance can be limited by the central device's bandwidth capacity.

flashcards.front: How do the OSI and TCP/IP models relate, and why do we use layered network models?
flashcards.back: The OSI 7-layer model is a conceptual framework showing ideal network functions, while TCP/IP is the actual protocol suite used in the Internet. Both use layering to: standardize communication between diverse systems, allow modular development (one layer can be updated without affecting others), simplify troubleshooting by isolating problems, and enable interoperability between different hardware and software vendors.
